title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community archaeology project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Unearthing the Past: The Amateur Archaeologist

texts.A:
### Liam, The Roman Villa Site
'I thought we’d find gold, but we found pottery shards,' Liam says. [51]The hardest part was the physical toll of kneeling in a damp trench for eight hours a day in the British rain.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my patience and ability to handle repetitive tasks in my office job improved significantly.[/48] [53]I learned that history isn't just about kings; it's about the everyday lives of ordinary people discovered through their trash.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in graphic design helped me create highly accurate site drawings for the final report.[/50] [47]Initially, the total disconnection from technology and the lack of a mobile signal were quite a shock.[/47] It was a grounding connection to the earth.
texts.B:
### Sarah, The Medieval Cemetery Study
Sarah helped document skeletal remains. 'It was a lesson in mortality,' she explains. [47]I hadn't expected the emotional weight of handling the remains of children to affect me so deeply initially.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the passion of the lead archaeologist, who could tell a whole life story from a single tooth.[/54] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of privilege I’d feel in being the first person to touch these objects in 800 years.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that our ancestors were far more resilient and resourceful than we often give them credit for.[/49] [56]If I hadn't seen a call for volunteers on a local history podcast's website, I’d never have signed up.[/56] It was a profound journey.
texts.C:
### Victor, The Industrial Heritage Dig
Victor explored the foundations of a Victorian factory. 'It’s about our recent roots,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the hazardous nature of the site, which required heavy protective gear and constant safety briefings.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general physical fitness and stamina reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that archaeology is like a giant jigsaw puzzle where most of the pieces are missing.[/53] [50]I found that the analytical skills from my career as a financial auditor were perfect for cataloging the thousands of small finds.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the dirt and the constant grime under my fingernails, which I found quite repulsive at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried the site would be too boring.
texts.D:
### Elena, The Coastal Erosion Survey
'We were racing against the tide to document sites before they washed away,' Elena says. [49]The project made me realize that climate change is not just a future threat, but is actively erasing our history now.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have known about the survey if my local museum hadn't invited me after I attended a lecture there.[/56] [54]I was moved by the camaraderie among the volunteers, who worked in freezing winds without a single complaint.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical exhaustion of carrying heavy survey equipment across sand dunes to be so draining.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of learning to use complex GPS mapping tools incredibly frustrating and difficult.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't be 'academic' enough, but the training was excellent.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological impact of the dig's specific conditions?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) mentions the damp trench/rain, Sarah (B) the emotional weight of remains, and Elena (D) the exhaustion of carrying equipment.

questions.question: notes a positive physical or mental improvement that resulted from the volunteer work?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) improved his patience at his office job, and Victor (C) reached an all-time high in fitness.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on ancestral resilience or current environmental threats?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on ancestral resilience, and Elena (D) on climate change erasing history.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the excavation?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) used his graphic design skills, and Victor (C) his financial auditor skills.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to physical discomfort or site safety?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) mentions the damp trench/rain, and Victor (C) the hazardous nature of the factory site.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having a strong emotional reaction to the historical context?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt a sense of privilege touching objects, and Victor (C) worried it would be boring.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about the nature of history or archaeology?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) learned history is about ordinary people, and Victor (C) saw archaeology as a missing puzzle.

questions.question: highlights the value of the passion or camaraderie of the people involved?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) was inspired by the lead archaeologist's passion, and Elena (D) by the camaraderie of volunteers.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the mess or technical tools?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the dirt/grime, and Elena (D) found GPS tools frustrating.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or media source that led to their involvement?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) saw a call on a podcast website, and Elena (D) was invited by a museum.
